message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this plan through ONE lens. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Check /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board and /home/polaris/wt/flywheel.

PLAN DIAGNOSIS: undefined
RETRIEVAL: undefined
SYNTHESIS LANE: undefined
COMPOSER: undefined
EXPECTED: undefined

LENS:
BAR-LOWERING: does this plan actually aim at BEATING 0.5578, or has it quietly retreated to parity / bodhi / "a good improvement"? Every previous plan did exactly that. Quote the place where it settles for less, or confirm it does not.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# BRIEF V4 — THE MISSION IS TO **BEAT** 0.5578. ONE SINGLE PLAN. UNLEASH EVERYTHING.
2	
3	## 0. THE OPERATOR'S DIRECTION (this is the frame; do not narrow it)
4	> "Why stick with bodhi class? Why not the top one? ... If we have any old rules that hurt us from score, just
5	> drop it. If we don't have enough corpus to make us SOTA, then kick on the agentic outline search, or we do
6	> better at the early query generation + search + fetch + select + weight + dedup + consolidate, make them
7	> much much much better and richer so we are SOTA. Don't stuck with anything. I need one single solid plan,
8	> unleash the full power at the beginning, then keep the hamster wheel running till we match SOTA and beat SOTA."
9	
10	**TARGET: > 0.5578 (cellcog-max, #1). NOT parity. NOT bodhi. THE TOP.**
11	Every previous plan (mine, Fable's, Sol's) quietly lowered the bar to match what had already been built.
12	**That is the failure mode to avoid. Design for #1.**
13	
14	## 1. THE BOARD (task 72, our evaluator = GPT-5.5, from the benchmark's own per-task raw_results)
15	| system | OVERALL | compreh | INSIGHT | instr-follow | readability |
16	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
17	| **cellcog-max** | **0.5578** (corpus-wide #1; no per-task breakdown published — its task-72 score may be HIGHER than bodhi's) | 0.5634 | 0.5708 | 0.5530 | 0.5194 |
18	| bodhi (best *known* task-72 score) | 0.5441 | 0.5441 | 0.5457 | 0.5559 | 0.5199 |
19	| lunon | 0.5406 | | 0.5594 | | |
20	| WhaleCloud | 0.5396 | | | | |
21	| gemini-2.5-pro-DR = **THE REFERENCE** (parity=0.50) | 0.5102 | 0.5111 | 0.5035 | 0.5187 | 0.5100 |
22	| openai-DR | 0.4743 | | | | |
23	| **POLARIS** | **0.4382** | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | **0.3774** (worst on the entire board) |
24	Weights: **INSIGHT 0.32 | COMPREHENSIVENESS 0.29 | INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING 0.25 | READABILITY 0.14.**
25	Measurement: judge SD **0.0074**; k=5 paired => **smallest resolvable effect +0.0094**. NEVER score n=1 again.
26	
27	## 2. THE THREE CAUSES OF THE GAP (all measured, none disputed)
28	**A. THE JUDGE CANNOT SEE A SINGLE ONE OF OUR SOURCES.**
29	RACE runs an LLM cleaner that deletes every `[n]` marker and the entire reference list BEFORE judging.
30	MEASURED on 12 real facts x 5 citation formats through the PRODUCTION cleaner:
31	| format | authors survive | journals |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| `[1]` markers (**WHAT WE DO — all 240 of them**) | **0/12** | 0/12 |
34	| `(Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2019)` — the "obvious" fix | **0/12** | 0/12 |
35	| `Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019) show...` | 5/12 | 0/12 |
36	| **`Writing in the JEP in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show...`** | **10/12** | **12/12** |
37	**It is the JOURNAL NAME that anchors the clause as prose. And the year MUST be prose — every one of
38	cellcog's 281 parenthetical `(YYYY)` is deleted.** What the judge reads from us today:
39	*"About 47 percent of total US employment is at risk of computerisation."* — a naked, unattributed
40	assertion. That is Frey & Osborne, 5,223 citations, peer-reviewed. **We did the scholarly work and hid
41	every trace of it.** On a task whose instruction is *"only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles."*
42	
43	**B. WE CANNOT REASON ACROSS SOURCES — BY CONSTRUCTION.**
44	`entailment_judge.py:588` NEUTRAL clause deletes any sentence introducing *"a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT
45	present in the SPAN"*. A cross-source inference IS that. **98% of our sentences are bare facts; bodhi's are ~45%
46	analysis; cellcog's synthesis prose is most of the document.** INSIGHT is 0.32 — the heaviest weight — and our worst.
47	
48	**C. OUR REPORT IS PHYSICALLY UNREADABLE.**
49	12 paragraphs, **median 677 words**, ZERO H3. Every other system on the board — *including the two we beat* —
50	sits at 38-170 words/paragraph. **Our readability (0.3774) is the WORST SCORE ON THE BOARD.** And the judge scores
51	all ~25 criteria in ONE call, writing a comparative analysis BEFORE the numbers => **cross-dimension bleed is real:
52	our wall-of-text impression taxes the other 86% of the score.**
53	
54	## 3. **THE FAITHFULNESS LINE — CORRECTED. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION.**
55	I previously told the operator there was a hard trade-off: *"bodhi wins by writing sentences no single source
56	supports; we cannot match that without fabricating."* **THAT WAS WRONG.** Look at what bodhi actually writes:
57	
58	> *"...aggregate impacts were too small to detect over the period studied **[3]** — a pattern consistent with GPT
59	> diffusion lags and within-firm reorganization preceding macro reallocation **[1]**."*
60	
61	**IT CITES TWO SOURCES.** [3] is the empirical null. [1] is the GPT/productivity-paradox paper **WHICH STATES THE
62	DIFFUSION-LAG MECHANISM**. **bodhi is not inventing a mechanism. It is citing a THEORY paper for the mechanism and
63	an EMPIRICAL paper for the finding, and connecting them. THAT IS NOT HALLUCINATION — THAT IS WHAT A LITERATURE
64	REVIEW IS.**
65	
66	Our gate kills that sentence for a stupid reason: it takes the UNION of the cited spans and asks *"is every
67	assertion literally present?"* The composite claim is verbatim in neither — **even though BOTH ITS COMPONENTS ARE
68	FULLY GROUNDED IN THE TWO PAPERS IT CITES.**
69	
70	**THE CORRECTED LINE — design to this:**
71	- **FABRICATING A FACT** — a number, a study, a finding, an attribution that does not exist. **FRAUD. NEVER.**
72	- **CONNECTING GROUNDED CLAIMS** — a finding from paper A + a mechanism stated by paper B, with BOTH CITED.
73	  **SCHOLARSHIP. ALWAYS ALLOWED.**
74	**A mechanism may be asserted if ANY source in the corpus states it AND we cite that source.** (The contract at
75	`scripts/synthesis_contract.py` currently requires the mechanism to be stated by the two papers being compared —
76	**too strict, it forbids exactly bodhi's winning move. FIX IT.**)
77	**STILL DELETED, DETERMINISTICALLY:** any number not in a source | any study/organisation that does not exist |
78	any attribution to someone who did not say it | any mechanism NO paper anywhere states | forecasts | universals.
79	
80	**DROP ANY OTHER RULE THAT COSTS US SCORE AND BUYS US NOTHING.** The operator's instruction is explicit. Audit the
81	pipeline for rules that are pure cost: the 15-sources-per-section rule (unsatisfiable, silently padded), the
82	tier gate, the `[n]` marker convention itself (invisible to the judge — the bibliography can STAY for provenance,
83	but the PROSE must carry the attribution).
84	
85	## 4. THE CORPUS IS THE OTHER HALF — AND OUR RETRIEVAL IS AIMED WRONG
86	MEASURED: `data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.corrected.json` = **997 evidence rows, 919 distinct URLs, 530 domains,
87	206 DOIs, 107 journals — and EVERY row already carries a direct quote.** But:
88	- **only 5 rows had AUTHOR NAMES** (which is why in-prose attribution was impossible);
89	- after Crossref enrichment, **120 distinct journal articles — of which only 17 ARE ON-TOPIC.** The rest is
90	  **ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist, Cognitive Psychology 1974 (reading automaticity), Journal of Finance 1996.**
91	  **Prestigious, peer-reviewed, and completely irrelevant.**
92	- Top domains retrieved: researchgate.net (57), bls.gov (29), oecd.org (20), ssrn (12), nber (12) — **grey literature.**
93	**WE DID NOT HAVE A CORPUS PROBLEM. WE HAD AN AIMING PROBLEM.**
94	
95	**cellcog has ~98 ON-TOPIC canonical journal articles. We have 17 (+32 I hand-built tonight by citation-graph
96	expansion) = ~45. THAT IS NOT ENOUGH AND WE MUST NOT SETTLE FOR IT.**
97	
98	**WE HAVE AN AGENTIC RETRIEVAL LOOP WE NEVER USED FOR THIS.** (`PG_AGENTIC_*`: max 15 rounds, 120 queries,
99	convergence on theme saturation + URL overlap; `outline_agent`; the whole query-gen -> search -> fetch -> select ->
100	weight -> dedup -> consolidate chain.) **I spent hours doing MANUAL API pulls while the system's own search agent
101	sat idle. Fix the retrieval AT THE ROOT and run it to SATURATION against a COVERAGE MATRIX, not to a number I picked.**
102	
103	PROVEN TONIGHT: **keyword search cannot find this literature.** Autor-Levy-Murnane (2003, QJE, 4,743 cites) is the
104	most important paper in the field and its title — *"The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change"* — contains
105	**neither "AI" nor "labor market"**. No keyword query finds it. **CITATION-GRAPH EXPANSION does.** Build that in.
106	
107	## 5. WHAT #1 LOOKS LIKE (measured from cellcog's own task-72 artifact)
108	| | cellcog (#1) | POLARIS today |
109	|---|---|---|
110	| on-topic journal sources | ~98 | 22 |
111	| body words | 13,580 | 7,742 |
112	| structure | 9 H2 + **31 H3 + 8 H4** | 11 H2, **0 H3** |
113	| median paragraph | ~100w | **677w** |
114	| in-prose attributions | **133 author-year + 65 journal names** | **~0 visible** |
115	| epistemic labels | **14** (`[Established finding]`) — **THE ONLY SYSTEM ON THE BOARD THAT DOES THIS** | none |
116	| structured abstract | Objective / Methods / Findings / Contributions — **survives the cleaner in full** | none |
117	| **"Scope, Methods, and Source Selection" section** | **narrates its journal-only criteria IN PROSE — survives the cleaner 100% — it EXPLAINS ITS COMPLIANCE TO THE GRADER**, even pre-empting the working-paper objection | **we say nothing** |
118	| analysis vs reporting | ~half analysis | **2%** |
119	| `[n]` markers | 0 | 240 |
120	**"4IR" secretly means "COMPARE AI TO THE PREVIOUS THREE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTIONS"** — ~11.5% of the score across
121	three criteria. We drop it after paragraph one.
122	**Our report OPENS with meta-commentary about itself** (*"This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence
123	on the question above"* — there is no question above) — **and the judge reads the opening and closing most carefully.**
124	
125	## 6. **HOW WE BEAT cellcog, NOT JUST MATCH IT**
126	cellcog's claims are ASSERTED. **OURS CAN BE VERIFIED — every sentence traceable to a span in a real paper.**
127	The rubric explicitly grades **"Data and Factual Support: provides sufficient data, facts, cases, or evidence to
128	support its arguments and analysis"** (comprehensiveness) and **"Depth and Representativeness of Literature
129	Synthesized"**. **That is a criterion we can OUT-SCORE cellcog on, not merely match.**
130	**THE PLAY: cellcog's document architecture + cellcog's adjudication + DEEPER EVIDENCE THAN cellcog + grounding
131	cellcog does not have.** That is a #1 play. Copying bodhi never was.
132	
133	## 7. WHAT IS ALREADY BUILT (reuse it; fix what is wrong)
134	- `scripts/corpus_audit.py` / `enrich_bibliography.py` / `corpus_enrich_merge.py` — Crossref enrichment (works).
135	- `scripts/journal_corpus_build.py` — citation-graph expansion from canonical anchors (works; **this is how you
136	  find the real literature**).
137	- `scripts/journal_corpus_fetch.py` + `deep_fetch.py` — Crossref abstracts + Unpaywall OA full text.
138	- `scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py` — **proved which citation format survives. Reuse the finding.**
139	- `scripts/synthesis_contract.py` — typed adjudication, 14/14, **ZERO false admissions. But its mechanism rule is
140	  TOO STRICT (see §3) — fix it to allow a mechanism stated by ANY cited source.**
141	- `scripts/cellcog_composer.py` — first cut of the composer (evidence cards -> attributed, adjudicated prose).
142	- Baseline pinned: rank10 = **0.4382** (k=5, SD 0.0074).
143	
144	## 8. YOUR TASK — ONE SINGLE, COMPLETE, EXECUTABLE PLAN TO **BEAT 0.5578**
145	Cover, end to end:
146	1. **RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT** — query generation, search, fetch, select, weight, dedup, consolidate. Journal-first.
147	   Citation-graph expansion. A COVERAGE MATRIX as the stopping condition (every restructuring dimension and every
148	   industry the task names). How many papers, and how do we know when we have enough?
149	2. **THE EVIDENCE LAYER** — cards with verbatim spans + declared fields (level/horizon/method/mechanism), canonical
150	   dedup, quality weighting.
151	3. **THE SYNTHESIS LANE** — the corrected faithfulness line. Typed adjudication. What EXACTLY may a sentence assert?
152	4. **THE COMPOSER** — cellcog's architecture: H2/H3/H4, ~100w paragraphs, in-prose journal-named attribution,
153	   epistemic labels, structured abstract, Scope-and-Methods section, 4IR as an organising spine, industry coverage.
154	5. **WHAT OLD RULES TO DROP** — name them, with the evidence that they cost score and buy nothing.
155	6. **THE WHEEL** — fix -> compose -> score k=5 -> read the prose line by line -> fix again. What is the loop, and
156	   what is the stopping condition?
157	7. **HONEST CEILING + KILL RULES.** If your plan does not beat 0.5578, say so and name exactly what is missing.
158	
159	Ground every lever in a VERBATIM rubric criterion or a MEASURED artifact fact. Where uncertain, SEARCH (papers,
160	GitHub, and the artifacts at `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/`).
161	**No proxies. We lost an entire night to those.**
162

1	# Verdict
2	
3	POLARIS cannot reach the cellcog profile by rewriting the banked report. It needs:
4	
5	1. A journal-only, canonical evidence corpus.
6	2. Source-level evidence cards rather than isolated spans.
7	3. A dual-lane composer that separates factual claims from premise-linked synthesis.
8	4. Narrative author/journal attribution that survives cleaning.
9	5. An outline-first renderer producing roughly 34 claim-first H3s and 12–15k cleaned body words.
10	
11	My estimated outcome for the complete redesign is **0.53–0.56**, centered near **0.55**. Bodhi parity is credible; 0.5578 is an aggressive but defensible target. Attribution alone will not get close.
12	
13	## Corrections to the brief
14	
15	Several premises do not survive inspection.
16	
17	- **POLARIS does not currently cite 97 journal articles.** It has 105 numbered bibliography rows and approximately 97 canonical sources after duplicates, but many are working papers, institutional reports, news articles, blogs, vendor pages, and mirrors. Examples include WEF, MIT Sloan, NBER, IZA, arXiv, OECD, Harvard Gazette, Substack, Toptal, IMF working papers, Goldman Sachs, vendor statistics, and J.P. Morgan. The report itself says only 6% of its material is T1 primary research. Retrieval depth is therefore not yet journal-literature depth.
18	
19	- **The cheap attribution transform is conditional.** Narratively attributing the current corpus would expose sources such as WEF, Goldman Sachs, Toptal, and Substack more clearly, potentially lowering “Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles.” Corpus filtering must precede—or at least gate—attribution.
20	
21	- **RACE cleaning is model-driven, not deterministic.** The cleaner sends each article or chunk to an LLM with instructions to remove citation marks, links, bibliographies, and footnotes. The observed local result may indeed contain zero markers, but that is an empirical output, not a regex guarantee. [The cleaner prompt says exactly this](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/blob/main/prompt/clean_prompt.py).
22	
23	- **“The judge sees no sources at all” is too strong.** It loses the numbered markers and reference list, but retains existing narrative references. POLARIS already names Acemoglu, Restrepo, Autor, Frey, Osborne, Felten, Raj, Seamans, Brynjolfsson, and several others. It also names at least *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, *Strategic Management Journal*, and *Science*. The sourcing signal is very weak, not literally zero.
24	
25	- **The table’s “10 authors / 1 journal” labels are not reproducible literally.** The raw POLARIS text contains more than twenty distinct author surnames and at least three journal venues. Those numbers may come from a narrower attribution-pattern regex, but then the metric needs that definition.
26	
27	- **The marker counts are reconcilable:** approximately 240 body occurrences plus 105 bibliography labels gives the reported 345 items entering cleaning.
28	
29	- **“No credit for being a better 7” is false as written.** Scores are continuous. A 7.8 beats a 7.0. Crossing 8 may be especially salient to the judge, but it is not a hard threshold. The bands are guidance, not quantization. [The scoring prompt explicitly uses continuous 0–10 scores](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/blob/main/prompt/score_prompt_en.py).
30	
31	- **Cellcog’s synthesis is not entirely fact-free.** “The task-based framework subsumes…” and “the earlier frameworks did not anticipate…” are theoretical assertions. They can be faithful when derived from already grounded premises, but they are more than stylistic glue. The safe target is not “unverified prose with no numbers”; it is **typed, premise-linked adjudication**.
32	
33	- Cellcog has exactly **31 H3 headings** by direct heading enumeration. POLARIS has zero. Cellcog’s bibliography contains about 104 rows; “98 distinct sources” appears to mean distinct body-cited works, not bibliography entries.
34	
35	The grader facts do check out: task 72 has 25 criteria—6 comprehensiveness, 5 insight, 7 instruction-following, and 7 readability—with weights 0.29/0.32/0.25/0.14. The complete task-specific rubric is in [criteria.jsonl](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/blob/main/data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl). Both articles and all criteria are presented to one comparative scoring call, and the final calculation is target/(target+reference), as implemented in [deepresearch_bench_race.py](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/blob/main/deepresearch_bench_race.py).
36	
37	## The required architecture
38	
39	```text
40	retrieval
41	  ↓
42	canonical journal-only corpus
43	  ↓
44	verified source cards: study + setting + method + result + limitation
45	  ↓
46	rubric/coverage matrix
47	  ↓
48	34 claim-first H3 plans, each with premises and an adjudication question
49	  ↓
50	dual-lane generation
51	  ├─ EVIDENCE sentence → existing single/multi-span provenance gate
52	  └─ SYNTHESIS sentence → new premise-operation contract
53	  ↓
54	75-word paragraph renderer + narrative attribution
55	  ↓
56	cleaner-survival audit
57	  ↓
58	report + machine-readable sentence ledger
59	```
60	
61	### Code placement
62	
63	I would make these changes:
64	
65	- `provenance_generator.py`, at the existing `verify_sentence_provenance` definition reported near line 2098:
66	  - Keep the current zero-failure behavior for `sentence_kind="evidence"`.
67	  - Dispatch `sentence_kind="synthesis"` to a separate validator.
68	  - Update every caller of `verify_sentence_provenance`; do not silently infer the sentence kind.
69	
70	- `entailment_judge.py`, around the NEUTRAL rule reported near line 588:
71	  - **Do not weaken this rule.**
72	  - Its refusal of unsupported facts, entities, and mechanisms is correct for evidence sentences.
73	  - A global relaxation would sell the faithfulness moat.
74	
75	- Add `evidence_cards.py:1`:
76	  - Canonical DOI/source deduplication.
77	  - Journal/language/peer-review verification.
78	  - Source-card construction and coverage-matrix generation.
79	
80	- Add `synthesis_contract.py:1`:
81	  - Typed synthesis operations, premise validation, entity/number checks, and an adversarial test suite.
82	
83	- Add `narrative_attribution.py:1`:
84	  - Deterministic rendering from validated `authors`, `year`, and `venue`.
85	  - First-mention and later-mention policies.
86	  - Cleaner-survival auditing.
87	
88	- Add `cellcog_composer.py:1`:
89	  - Outline-first section planning, paragraph budgeting, source allocation, and typed sentence generation.
90	  - This replaces the current report-to-report composition path; it should not post-edit `rank10_sections_compose/report.md`.
91	
92	Because the managed shell failed before command execution, I could not independently verify the two private-code line numbers supplied in the brief or locate the current compose-prompt line. I therefore use exact symbol anchors and new-file line 1 rather than inventing line numbers. Locate integration sites with:
93	
94	```bash
95	rg -n "def verify_sentence_provenance|verify_sentence_provenance\\(" /home/polaris/polaris_project
96	rg -n "len\\(failures\\).*0|NEUTRAL" /home/polaris/polaris_project
97	rg -n "This report synthesizes|Cross-Study Synthesis|Additional Corroborated" /home/polaris/polaris_project
98	```
99	
100	## Lever 1: Repair the corpus before composing
101	
102	The existing source count is a false strength. Build a **90–105 source corpus of canonical, verified English journal articles**, not 97 heterogeneous web records.
103	
104	Positive admission should require:
105	
106	- Canonical DOI or publisher record.
107	- Publication type verified as journal article.
108	- English-language title/full text or explicit language metadata.
109	- Named journal and authors.
110	- Peer-review status or reputable journal/index evidence.
111	- At least one usable full-context evidence span.
112	- No canonical duplicate by DOI/title.
113	
114	Reject reports, working papers, proceedings, books, news, explainers, consultancy forecasts, blogs, repositories, mirrors, and vendor pages from the evidence used in the review. They may be stored as excluded context, but never cited or used as factual premises.
115	
116	Each admitted article needs a card such as:
117	
118	```json
119	{
120	  "source_id": "...",
121	  "authors": ["..."],
122	  "year": 2025,
123	  "journal": "...",
124	  "doi": "...",
125	  "study_type": "field experiment",
126	  "level": "worker",
127	  "industry": "customer service",
128	  "geography": "United States",
129	  "horizon": "...",
130	  "outcomes": ["productivity", "attrition"],
131	  "verified_claim_ids": ["..."],
132	  "limitations": ["..."],
133	  "metadata_receipts": ["..."]
134	}
135	```
136	
137	“Study type,” “level,” “outcomes,” and “limitations” must themselves be span-supported.
138	
139	| Rubric moved | Expected raw movement |
140	|---|---:|
141	| “Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized” | +0.6 to +1.2 |
142	| “Exclusive Citation of ‘High-Quality Journal Articles’” | +1.5 to +2.5 |
143	| “Exclusive Citation of ‘English-Language’ Journal Articles” | +0.2 to +0.5 |
144	| “Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation…” | +0.3 to +0.7 |
145	
146	Failure mode: treating the existing tier label as proof of journal quality. The current data already labels some explainer pages implausibly highly.
147	
148	Cheap pre-compose test: produce a corpus audit CSV. Release requires:
149	
150	- 100% admitted sources with positive journal and English evidence.
151	- Zero nonjournal types.
152	- Zero DOI/title duplicates.
153	- At least 80 sources with usable method/result/limitation cards.
154	- Coverage of at least six industries and all six required restructuring outcomes.
155	
156	## Lever 2: Narrative attribution that survives RACE
157	
158	Do not simply replace `[1]` with `(Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2019)`. The cleaner is instructed to remove complex citation formats too. Use grammatical attribution:
159	
160	> In their 2019 *Journal of Economic Perspectives* article, Acemoglu and Restrepo distinguish displacement from reinstatement.
161	
162	Rules:
163	
164	- First use: year + journal + all surnames up to four.
165	- Later use: surnames + year, unless the cleaner test shows the year is removed.
166	- Multiple sources: integrate them as actors in a comparison, not as a parenthetical block.
167	- Preserve `[n]` markers and the bibliography for provenance/FACT; narrative attribution is an additional RACE-surviving channel.
168	- Only attribute sources admitted by the journal firewall.
169	- Every surname, year, and venue must match the canonical metadata record.
170	- Missing author or venue means “do not render,” not “guess.”
171	
172	| Rubric moved | Expected raw movement |
173	|---|---:|
174	| “Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized” | +0.3 to +0.7 |
175	| “Exclusive Citation of ‘High-Quality Journal Articles’” | +0.5 to +1.0 |
176	| “P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information” | +0.3 to +0.6 |
177	| “L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone” | +0.2 to +0.5 |
178	
179	Failure mode: repetitive “X found…” prose that remains a catalogue, or attribution that exposes bad sources.
180	
181	Cheap test: make a 20-sentence fixture with five forms—numbered markers, parenthetical author-year, possessive attribution, “writing in Journal” attribution, and lexical year/journal attribution. Clean it five times with the production cleaner. Choose the form with:
182	
183	- 100% author and journal survival.
184	- No changed factual text.
185	- No orphaned grammatical fragments.
186	
187	Then apply journal-only narrative attribution to the banked report and score the exact before/after pair five times. Require at least **+0.0094 overall** before treating it as a scored lever.
188	
189	## Lever 3: Add a premise-linked synthesis lane
190	
191	The central change is not “allow NEUTRAL sentences.” It is to give synthesis a different proof obligation.
192	
193	Every generated sentence must declare one kind:
194	
195	### Evidence
196	
197	A factual statement about a study, entity, number, mechanism, finding, or method. It continues through the current provenance gate and ships only with zero failures.
198	
199	### Synthesis
200	
201	A relationship over already admitted premises. It must declare:
202	
203	```json
204	{
205	  "sentence_kind": "synthesis",
206	  "operation": "CONTRAST_LEVEL",
207	  "premise_ids": ["claim_17", "claim_42"],
208	  "draft": "Firm-level expansion can coexist with worker-level losses because the two estimates observe different adjustment margins."
209	}
210	```
211	
212	Allowed operations:
213	
214	- `CONJUNCTION`
215	- `COMPARE_DIRECTION`
216	- `CONTRAST_LEVEL`
217	- `CONTRAST_HORIZON`
218	- `BOUNDARY_CONDITION`
219	- `RANK_EVIDENCE`
220	- `CONSENSUS_OR_DISAGREEMENT`
221	- `COVERAGE_GAP`
222	- `CONDITIONAL_IMPLICATION`
223	
224	Validation rules:
225	
226	1. Every named entity and numeral must occur in a premise or canonical metadata.
227	2. Causal language is forbidden unless a premise contains the mechanism and supports causal interpretation.
228	3. Evidence ranking may use only declared fields such as design, level, horizon, sample, and directness.
229	4. A coverage gap must correspond to an empty or underpopulated coverage-matrix cell.
230	5. A conditional implication may recommend investigation or policy attention but cannot predict an unobserved effect.
231	6. “May reflect X” fails if X is not a supported mechanism.
232	7. The rendered sentence is rechecked against its structured plan; divergence deletes it.
233	
234	This permits:
235	
236	> The frameworks are complementary because one organizes relative skill demand while the other explains endogenous task allocation.
237	
238	It does not permit:
239	
240	> The difference probably reflects slower regional adoption.
241	
242	unless adoption speed is an explicit, grounded premise.
243	
244	| Rubric moved | Expected raw movement |
245	|---|---:|
246	| “Analytical Depth in Characterizing…Mechanisms” | +1.5 to +2.5 |
247	| “Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation…” | +1.5 to +2.5 |
248	| “Identification…of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives” | +1.5 to +2.5 |
249	| “Insightful Integration…within the 4IR Context” | +0.7 to +1.4 |
250	| “Value and Foresight…Future Research Agendas” | +0.8 to +1.5 |
251	
252	Failure mode: laundering a novel causal story as “analysis,” or allowing a multi-span NLI model to approve something merely plausible.
253	
254	Cheap test: create an adversarial suite from 50 existing source-card pairs:
255	
256	- 100 valid conjunctions/contrasts/rankings/gaps.
257	- 100 invalid examples that introduce mechanisms, causalize correlations, generalize local results nationally, change time horizons, or introduce entities/numbers.
258	
259	Release requires **zero false admissions** on the invalid set and at least 90% acceptance of the valid set, followed by manual review of every accepted example.
260	
261	## Lever 4: Replace section composition with claim modules
262	
263	The report should be planned as approximately 34 H3 modules:
264	
265	- 3: scope, method, and 4IR framing.
266	- 5: theoretical mechanisms.
267	- 8: technology eras and measurement.
268	- 6: industry comparisons.
269	- 6: wages, employment, labor share, skills, inequality, autonomy, and institutions.
270	- 4: cross-source adjudications and policy implications.
271	- 2: limitations/frontier agenda and conclusion.
272	
273	Every H3 title should make a proposition:
274	
275	- “Within-firm augmentation and platform displacement are institutionally compatible”
276	- “Exposure measures disagree because they encode different theories of susceptibility”
277	- “Employment stability can conceal labor-share and autonomy losses”
278	
279	Each module receives:
280	
281	- 3–6 source cards.
282	- At least two perspectives where the literature permits.
283	- One declared adjudication question.
284	- 350–450 cleaned words.
285	- Four to six paragraphs of roughly 60–100 words.
286	- An evidence → comparison → boundary → implication progression.
287	
288	Target the **cleaned artifact**, not raw Markdown:
289	
290	- 12,500–14,500 body words.
291	- 32–36 H3 headings.
292	- Median paragraph 70–95 words.
293	- 90–105 canonical journal sources.
294	- 7–10 surviving narrative author attributions per 1,000 words.
295	- 55–65% internally tagged synthesis/adjudication sentences.
296	
297	Those are activation constraints, not score proxies. Only repeated RACE evaluation establishes benefit.
298	
299	| Rubric moved | Expected raw movement |
300	|---|---:|
301	| “Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis” | +0.8 to +1.5 |
302	| “Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered” | +0.4 to +0.9 |
303	| “S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization” | +1.5 to +2.5 |
304	| “S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions” | +2.0 to +3.0 |
305	| “P1: Clarity and Synthesis…” | +1.0 to +2.0 |
306	| “F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency” | +1.0 to +2.0 |
307	
308	Failure mode: manufacturing 34 headings that simply subdivide the existing fact conveyor. A module without an adjudication question and premise-linked synthesis should fail planning.
309	
310	Cheap test: generate the outline and module plans without prose. The linter must prove:
311	
312	- Every rubric concept has assigned modules.
313	- Every H3 contains an explicit claim.
314	- Every source card has a planned use.
315	- No module consists entirely of single-source exposition.
316	- Every proposed synthesis sentence already has premise IDs and an allowed operation.
317	
318	## Lever 5: Source-level deduplication and evidence adjudication
319	
320	The existing report repeats mirrors of the same study and even emits “(also mirrored).” Canonicalize before planning, then compare studies on meaningful dimensions:
321	
322	- Worker, firm, platform, regional, or national level.
323	- Experimental, quasi-experimental, observational, qualitative, or meta-analytic design.
324	- Exposure versus realized outcome.
325	- Short versus longer horizon.
326	- Employment, wages, productivity, labor share, skills, or autonomy.
327	- Institutional setting and industry.
328	
329	This is how POLARIS can explain apparent contradictions without inventing a mechanism. For example, firm growth and worker losses are not automatically contradictory when they use different observational levels. The safe analytical statement is about estimands and scope; a causal institutional explanation needs a grounded source.
330	
331	| Rubric moved | Expected raw movement |
332	|---|---:|
333	| “Balanced Discussion of AI’s Labor Market Impacts” | +0.5 to +1.0 |
334	| “Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation…” | +1.0 to +1.8 |
335	| “D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized” | +0.6 to +1.2 |
336	| “P1: Clarity and Synthesis…” | +0.8 to +1.5 |
337	
338	Failure mode: ranking evidence solely by journal prestige or pipeline tier rather than study design and fit to the claim.
339	
340	Cheap test: select five apparent contradictions already in the bank. The system must produce a comparison grid and a bounded adjudication for each, with no new entity, number, or causal mechanism.
341	
342	## Rollout and measurement
343	
344	Do not spend a full compose until all mechanism tests pass.
345	
346	1. **Corpus audit**
347	   - Canonicalize and classify all 105 existing rows.
348	   - This will reveal the real retrieval deficit.
349	
350	2. **Cleaner-survival experiment**
351	   - Five cleaner runs per attribution form.
352	   - Then five paired RACE runs on the journal-only attribution retrofit.
353	
354	3. **Synthesis-gate adversarial suite**
355	   - No full prose generation.
356	   - Zero unsupported admissions is a release requirement.
357	
358	4. **Outline and two-module micro-compose**
359	   - One theory module and one cross-industry contradiction module.
360	   - Inspect the sentence ledger, not just the prose.
361	
362	5. **Full compose**
363	   - Run three independent compositions.
364	   - Score each artifact five times.
365	   - With generation SD ≈0.016, three composes reduce generation SE to about 0.0092; five judges reduce the per-artifact judge SE to about 0.0033. Combined uncertainty is roughly 0.010, so a two-sigma detectable integrated effect is around 0.02.
366	
367	6. **Release threshold**
368	   - Mean ≥0.5441 across independent compositions.
369	   - No composition below 0.52.
370	   - Zero faithfulness violations in sentence-ledger audit.
371	   - Cleaner-survival and corpus-admission checks remain hard gates.
372	
373	## Expected ceiling
374	
375	My non-additive estimate is:
376	
377	| Configuration | Expected RACE |
378	|---|---:|
379	| Journal-safe narrative attribution on banked prose | 0.45–0.48 |
380	| Journal corpus repair + current fact-conveyor composer | 0.47–0.51 |
381	| New dual-lane composer without complete corpus repair | 0.50–0.53 |
382	| Full corpus + cards + synthesis contract + profile renderer | **0.53–0.56** |
383	| Excellent execution with stable cleaner survival | **0.55–0.58** |
384	
385	The full redesign plausibly reaches bodhi and can reach cellcog, but 0.5578 is not guaranteed by matching paragraph length, heading count, or author density. The decisive mechanism is:
386	
387	> **Verified journal evidence made visible in prose, organized into bounded cross-source adjudications that the current single-span gate cannot express.**
388	
389	That is the path that preserves faithfulness while removing the actual reasoning bottleneck.

content.text: Checked the board artifacts and the latest plan on disk (`/home/polaris/polaris_project/sota_review/codex_plan_v3.md` — the only plan present; the orchestrator passed all plan fields as `undefined`, so I reviewed the newest candidate against `/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_BRIEF_V4.md`).

toolUseResult: Error: Output does not match required schema: root: must have required property 'refuted', root: must have required property 'reasoning'

input.reasoning:
REFUTED under the BAR-LOWERING lens. Two independent problems: (0) the plan handed to me was empty — every field (PLAN DIAGNOSIS / RETRIEVAL / SYNTHESIS LANE / COMPOSER / EXPECTED) arrived as "undefined", so under "default refuted=true if uncertain" it fails on its face. I therefore refuted the only plan artifact that actually exists on disk, /home/polaris/polaris_project/sota_review/codex_plan_v3.md, against /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_BRIEF_V4.md. It lowers the bar in five places, and I can quote each one.

1. THE SHIP GATE IS BODHI, VERBATIM. codex_plan_v3.md:368, "Rollout and measurement > Release threshold":
   "- Mean >=0.5441 across independent compositions."
   0.5441 is bodhi's task-72 overall (SOTA_BRIEF_V4.md:18). The mission is >0.5578 (BRIEF line 10: "TARGET: > 0.5578 (cellcog-max, #1). NOT parity. NOT bodhi. THE TOP."). The plan's own stopping condition therefore fires 0.0137 BELOW the target, at exactly the number the brief names as the thing not to settle for. A compose that scores 0.5450 passes release, the wheel stops, and the mission is missed by design. This is the single load-bearing quote: the plan does not aim at 0.5578, it ships at bodhi.
   Worse: cellcog's task-72 score is UNPUBLISHED (only the corpus-wide mean 0.5578 exists; I confirmed /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/scores/cellcog-max/race_result.txt contains only the 4 corpus-wide dimensions + Overall 0.5578, no per-task rows). Its task-72 score may well be above 0.5578. A gate at 0.5441 is not "close"; it is not even in the running.

2. THE EXPECTED OUTCOME IS CENTERED BELOW TARGET. Line 11: "My estimated outcome for the complete redesign is 0.53-0.56, centered near 0.55. Bodhi parity is credible; 0.5578 is an aggressive but defensible target." "Centered near 0.55" is a plan that expects to LOSE. And the ceiling table (382-383) makes it explicit: the full build — corpus + cards + synthesis contract + renderer — is priced at 0.53-0.56; the only row that clears 0.5578 is "Excellent execution with stable cleaner survival | 0.55-0.58", which attaches no mechanism, no lever, and no test. Beating #1 is left to a residual adjective.

3. EVERY QUANTITATIVE TARGET IS A CLONE SPEC OF cellcog, i.e. A TIE AT BEST. Lines 288-296 target the cleaned artifact at "12,500-14,500 body words" (cellcog: 13,580), "32-36 H3" (cellcog: 31), "median paragraph 70-95w" (cellcog: ~100w), "90-105 canonical journal sources" (cellcog: ~98). A perfect execution of this spec produces a cellcog look-alike, whose expected score is cellcog's score. There is no dimension on which the plan is engineered to EXCEED cellcog. BRIEF §6 (lines 125-131) specifies the out-scoring play — "cellcog's claims are ASSERTED. OURS CAN BE VERIFIED... 'Data and Factual Support'... a criterion we can OUT-SCORE cellcog on, not merely match" — and that play appears NOWHERE in the plan's five levers or in any rubric table. The plan's own "decisive mechanism" (line 387) is "verified journal evidence made visible in prose" — which is a parity mechanism (it gets us to what cellcog already does), not a surpass mechanism.

4. THE CORPUS GATE RETREATS BELOW cellcog TOO. Lever 1's release check (line 154) requires "At least 80 sources with usable method/result/limitation cards" and "at least six industries". cellcog has ~98 on-topic journal articles (BRIEF:108-119). The plan will pass its corpus gate 18 sources short of #1 while the brief says (line 96) "We have 17 (+32) = ~45. THAT IS NOT ENOUGH AND WE MUST NOT SETTLE FOR IT." Compounding it, line 114 rejects "reports, working papers, proceedings, books" wholesale — NBER/SSRN/IZA is where a large share of this literature lives, and cellcog itself pre-empts the working-paper objection in prose rather than excluding them (BRIEF:117). The plan's admission rule can only shrink the corpus, and it never states how many papers it expects to end with or how it knows it has enough — the COVERAGE MATRIX was supposed to be the stopping condition (BRIEF §8.1), and it appears in the diagram (line 46) but never gets a saturation criterion.

5. THE RETRIEVAL ROOT-FIX IS ABSENT. BRIEF §4 (lines 98-105) is unambiguous: the agentic retrieval loop (PG_AGENTIC_*, 15 rounds/120 queries, outline_agent) was never used, and "keyword search cannot find this literature" — Autor-Levy-Murnane (QJE 2003) contains neither "AI" nor "labor market" in its title; only CITATION-GRAPH EXPANSION finds it. codex_plan_v3.md's Lever 1 is a FILTER on the existing 105 rows ("Canonicalize and classify all 105 existing rows", line 347) — it audits what we already have. It never runs the agentic loop, never runs citation-graph expansion to saturation, and never adds a source. You cannot filter 45 on-topic papers into 98. The plan quietly redefines "retrieval at the root" as "corpus hygiene", which is the cheapest available reading of the hardest instruction.

CONFIRMING the lens's prediction: this is the same retreat every prior plan made. It is not a plan to beat 0.5578; it is a plan to reach bodhi, with a footnote that #1 "is aggressive but defensible."
input.fix:
To be a #1 plan rather than a bodhi plan, the plan must change in five specific ways — each is a concrete edit to codex_plan_v3.md, not a tone change:

1. RESET THE RELEASE THRESHOLD ABOVE THE TARGET, NOT AT PARITY. Replace "Mean >=0.5441" (line 368) with: mean >= 0.5670 over >=3 independent composes x k=5 judges. Rationale: cellcog's task-72 score is unpublished; only its corpus-wide 0.5578 is known, so the true bar may be higher. The plan's own combined SE is ~0.010, so a gate at 0.5578 has ~50% chance of actually being at or below cellcog. Gate at target + 1 sigma. Delete "No composition below 0.52" — that floor is a parity artifact.

2. NAME THE SURPASS LEVER AND PRICE IT. Add a Lever 6: "Out-score cellcog on Data and Factual Support / Depth and Representativeness." cellcog asserts; POLARIS can carry a verifiable span behind every claim. Concretely: a per-claim evidence density target (e.g. every H3 carries >=2 named-journal, named-author, numeric findings with study design stated), plus the "Scope, Methods, and Source Selection" prose section that narrates our journal-only criteria AND our verifiability (cellcog narrates only its criteria). This is the only lever in the whole design that can produce a POSITIVE delta vs #1 rather than a zero one. Every other lever must be relabeled honestly as "close the gap", not "win".

3. TURN THE CLONE SPEC INTO A DOMINATE SPEC. Sources: >= 110 on-topic canonical journal articles (vs cellcog ~98), and the corpus gate must be a COVERAGE MATRIX saturation condition (every restructuring dimension x every industry the task names has >=2 independent primary studies), not a source count. Structure: match cellcog's shape (H3 count, ~100w paragraphs, 13k words, epistemic labels, structured abstract) — that is table stakes and should be stated as such — but exceed on evidence per section.

4. ACTUALLY DO RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT. Lever 1 must ADD sources, not filter existing ones. Sequence: (a) seed with 10-15 canonical anchors (ALM 2003 QJE, Acemoglu-Restrepo, Autor, Frey-Osborne, Brynjolfsson-Li-Raymond, Felten-Raj-Seamans...); (b) run scripts/journal_corpus_build.py citation-graph expansion (forward+backward) to depth 2; (c) run the PG_AGENTIC_* loop (15 rounds / 120 queries) with journal-first query generation against the coverage matrix; (d) stop on matrix saturation, not on a picked number. Also drop the blanket working-paper rejection: admit high-citation NBER/SSRN/IZA papers as a separate, labeled tier and pre-empt the objection in the Scope section exactly as cellcog does — a wholesale reject shrinks us below #1 and buys nothing the rubric asks for ("high-quality, English-language journal articles" is graded by the judge reading prose, and cellcog wins that criterion while citing working papers).

5. ADD A KILL RULE THAT NAMES THE MISS. BRIEF §8.7 demanded an honest ceiling. Replace the "excellent execution -> 0.55-0.58" hand-wave with: "If, after full corpus saturation + dual-lane synthesis + cellcog-architecture compose, the k=5 mean is below 0.5578, the missing factor is X" — and force the plan to name X in advance (my candidate: the judge scores all 25 criteria in one comparative call, so cross-dimension bleed from readability is doing more work than any single lever, and the residual gap is likely instruction-following criteria we never read verbatim from criteria.jsonl). A plan with no named failure mode above parity is a plan that expects parity.

Until at least (1) and (2) are in the document, the plan should be sent back: its stopping condition is bodhi's score and it contains no mechanism whose success would put POLARIS above cellcog.
